articles.summary:
Corporate mandates for rapid AI adoption are creating organizational inefficiencies and technical debt, mirroring the failed industrial policies of the 1958 Great Leap Forward in modern business environments.

### Key Points
- Companies are forcing employees to build AI agents and workflows without establishing necessary evaluation pipelines, monitoring, or data infrastructure.
- Performance metrics are being manipulated to satisfy top-down mandates, leading to inflated productivity reports that lack real-world validation.
- The aggressive elimination of middle management and specialized roles, such as QA, is stripping organizations of critical institutional knowledge.
- Employees are increasingly creating "poison pill" AI skills that appear functional but contain hidden dependencies to ensure their own job security.
- High-profile attempts to replace established SaaS providers with internal AI tools have frequently failed, resulting in a return to traditional vendor solutions.

### Why it Matters
This trend of performative AI integration risks creating a fragile infrastructure of unmaintainable "demoware" that hides systemic complexity behind clean user interfaces. As organizations prioritize adoption metrics over actual utility, they face long-term operational risks when these poorly validated systems inevitably fail to meet production demands.

articles.title: A 115-Inch Mini LED TV Exists Now, and It Has 330Hz Gaming
articles.author: Rei Padla
articles.content:
Most people assume Mini LED is what you settle for when OLED isn’t in the budget. LG built that perception itself, spending over a decade positioning OLED as the standard for picture quality. But the 2026 QNED evo range suggests the company is done treating Mini LED as the second tier, starting with a screen so large it could double as a wall.
Price: TBD
Where to Buy: LG
So the real question is: can 13 years of OLED leadership actually change what a Mini LED TV can do?
The company just rolled out its refreshed QNED evo Mini LED TV series for 2026, topped by a new 115-inch QNED90 that pushes LG further into ultra-large territory. The lineup spans six model tiers, from the flagship QNED90 down through the QNED85, QNED82, QNED8M, QNED80, and the entry-level QNED70, with availability starting this month in Korea, the U.S., and Europe.
Richer color reproduction across most of the lineup
The headline technology this year is Dynamic QNED Color Pro, a color mapping system that LG says helps maintain rich and accurate tones even in bright HDR scenes. Intertek has certified the technology for achieving 100 percent Color Volume, meaning the color gamut coverage matches or exceeds the DCI-P3 color space. That certification matters for sports viewers and gamers who rely on consistent color across bright, fast-moving content.
Powering the top-tier models is LG’s Alpha 8 AI Processor Gen 3, which appears in the 115-inch QNED90, the QNED85, and the QNED82. With 13 years of OLED leadership behind it, LG says the AI processor brings its OLED light control skills to the QNED platform.
Precision dimming gets sharper on the flagship
The 115-inch LG QNED90 gets the most aggressive backlight treatment with Precision Dimming Ultra, which LG describes as managing thousands of local dimming zones. The system controls light output zone by zone, aiming for deeper blacks and cleaner highlights with stronger contrast in darker scenes. For a Mini LED panel at 115 inches, that level of zone-by-zone control is a welcome move, especially for keeping detail visible in dark scenes on a screen this large.
The QNED85 and QNED82 share the same Alpha 8 AI Processor Gen 3, though LG reserves the full Precision Dimming Ultra feature for the flagship screen. LG hasn’t detailed the specific dimming capabilities of the other models in the lineup.
AI processing tackles upscaling and sound
LG packed several AI-driven enhancements into the 2026 QNED evo range. AI Super Upscaling, available on the QNED90 and QNED85, uses deep learning to sharpen textures, edges, and fine detail when scaling lower-resolution content up to 4K. On a 115-inch display, upscaling quality shows more than it would on a 65-inch panel, so the processing power here matters.
AI Picture Pro identifies faces, bodies, and other on-screen elements to improve depth and detail. Dynamic Tone Mapping Pro analyzes each frame individually to adjust resolution, brightness, and clarity, and it’s available across the QNED90, QNED85, QNED82, and QNED8M models.
On the audio side, AI Sound Pro creates a virtual 11.1.2 channel surround sound effect through the TV’s built-in speakers. It won’t replace a real sound system, but for viewers who want surround sound without extra hardware, the processing makes audio feel more spread out and directional.
Gaming features target competitive players
The 115-inch QNED90 supports variable refresh rate (VRR) up to 165Hz, AMD FreeSync Premium, and Auto Low Latency Mode (ALLM). Motion Booster pushes the effective refresh rate to 330Hz on the flagship model, with LG saying it significantly reduces motion blur in fast-paced shooters and racing games where split-second responses matter.
LG Gaming Portal rounds out the gaming feature set, providing access to cloud gaming services and native web games directly from the TV. FreeSync Premium support extends across the QNED90, QNED85, QNED82, and QNED8M. LG says the combination of high refresh rates and low latency keeps rapid in-game action sharp and responsive, with Motion Booster reducing motion blur during the kind of split-second sequences where it matters most.
Sports fans get a single hub
Running on webOS 26, the 2026 QNED evo TVs include Sports Portal, a single hub for live content, scores, match schedules, and league standings. Sports Alert sends notifications for selected teams and leagues, covering score updates, match results, and upcoming games. An AI Concierge card displays key stats like match progress and game-result predictions directly on screen, helping viewers stay updated on multiple games at once.
The prediction feature is a notable addition. LG frames the AI Concierge card as a way to help viewers stay updated on multiple games at once, while Sports Portal lets fans check key information without switching apps or inputs.
Smart features and security
Voice ID recognizes individual users and loads personalized home screens automatically. AI Concierge offers suggestions based on what you’re watching. Built-in AI from Google Gemini and Microsoft Copilot lets users ask questions, look things up, and search for related content while watching.
LG Shield, a CES 2026 Innovation Award winner, uses strong encryption to keep user data and personalized experiences safe across the platform.
The full 2026 QNED evo lineup
The range breaks down across six model tiers: The 115-inch QNED90 sits at the top with Dynamic QNED Color Pro, Precision Dimming Ultra, AI Super Upscaling, 165Hz VRR, and 330Hz Motion Booster. The 100-inch QNED85 offers a theater-like experience with AI Super Upscaling and the Alpha 8 AI Processor Gen 3. The QNED82 shares the same processor. The QNED8M and QNED80 offer Mini LED clarity for everyday viewing, while the QNED70 provides what LG calls a balance of size, performance, and accessibility.
Price: TBD
Where to Buy: LG, Amazon (Older models)
LG’s U.S. product listing shows a 100-inch QNED84B model at $3,999.99, though it isn’t clear whether this corresponds directly to the QNED85 in LG’s global lineup or represents a separate regional variant. Pricing for the 115-inch QNED90 hasn’t been confirmed at the time of writing.
articles.summary:
LG has launched its 2026 QNED evo Mini LED TV series, headlined by a massive 115-inch flagship model featuring advanced AI processing and high-performance gaming capabilities for global markets.

### Key Points
- The flagship 115-inch QNED90 features Precision Dimming Ultra and a 165Hz variable refresh rate with 330Hz Motion Booster technology.
- The lineup is powered by the Alpha 8 AI Processor Gen 3, which utilizes deep learning for image upscaling and sound optimization.
- Dynamic QNED Color Pro technology provides 100 percent Color Volume, ensuring accurate color reproduction in bright HDR environments.
- New software features include the Sports Portal hub for live updates and AI-driven tools like Google Gemini and Microsoft Copilot integration.
- The series spans six tiers, ranging from the premium QNED90 down to the entry-level QNED70, with immediate availability in Korea, the U.S., and Europe.

### Why it Matters
This release signals a strategic shift for LG, which is positioning its Mini LED technology as a high-end competitor to its own long-standing OLED dominance. By integrating advanced AI processing and ultra-large screen sizes, LG aims to capture the premium home theater and gaming markets that prioritize brightness and scale.

articles.title: Full Interview: Mark Surman, president of Mozilla, on the future of the open web, AI agents, and Mozilla’s plans for India
articles.author: Nikhil Pahwa
articles.content:
Mark Surman, President of Mozilla, has long championed an open, privacy-respecting internet. In a conversation with MediaNama Editor Nikhil Pahwa on the sidelines of the India AI Summit 2026, Surman reflects on how the rapid rise of artificial intelligence is reshaping the foundations of the web and the institutions built to support it.
Surman discusses the evolving role of browsers in an agent-first world, introducing the idea of “portable private memory” to ensure user control, interoperability, and competition across AI systems. He also addresses the economic challenges facing the open web as AI-driven interfaces reshape how users access content.
The interview further explores the promise of open-source AI, the growing importance of small language models, and Mozilla’s efforts to diversify its revenue and re-engage with India’s developer and open-source communities, positioning the organization as a key player in shaping a more open and trustworthy AI ecosystem.
You can watch the full interview here
Here is the full excerpt of the interview.
Table of contents for the interview
- The future of the open web in an AI-first world
- AI, declining traffic, and the future of web business models
- Rising bot traffic and the need for new web architecture
- Whether an agentic web requires new infrastructure
- Whether browsers have a future in an AI agent-driven world
- Browser engines, competition, and the need to evolve
- Interoperability, agents, and the changing role of the browser
- Mozilla’s values in an AI-driven internet
- Search, AI, and Mozilla’s Revenue Risk
- Product Strategy and Organisational Shift
- Mozilla Ventures and the “Firefox-like” Model
- What is Open-Source AI?
- Licensing, Commons, and Corporate Use
- Mozilla’s Approach to Open AI Ecosystems
- Takeaways from the India AI Impact Summit
- Small Language Models vs Big AI
- Mozilla’s India Strategy and Re-engagement
- India’s Opportunity in the AI Transition
1. The future of the open web in an AI-first world
Nikhil Pahwa: Let’s start with an easy one. Is there a future for the open web in an agent first world?
Mark Surman: I absolutely think there’s a future for the open web, but we actually have to figure out what that means and how to shape that future.
The web has gone through a lot of different evolutions, right? I mean, we could have been, in fact, we were, at the beginning of the smartphone revolution, saying, ‘Well, apps destroy the open web.’ Well, have they?
No. I mean, we certainly spend a lot of time in apps, but we also have found other ways to reach each other. We find the web in relation to apps. Often, content from the web we experience through apps; often, monetization comes through apps. But this web of content and relationships that is, I think, at the heart of what we mean by the open web still has continued to grow and grow.
So I think it will transform in AI. It could be destroyed. But now the question is, how do we keep what we mean and what we like about the open web alive? And that’s very much on my mind.
2. AI, declining traffic, and the future of web business models
Nikhil Pahwa: So one of the things that I’m hearing from publishers across the world, and now, in fact, even from the classifieds business, is that traffic is dropping sharply. So unlike the app ecosystem, where you had your own app and you still retained value, the shift that’s taking place now is that because of AI and the ability to substitute the content on the web, traffic is dropping. And if those business models collapse, then the ability for people to create independent content services collapses.
So, I mean, going back to that same question again: what does the web become? Because it sounds like it’s becoming a source of content for AI rather than AI becoming a source of traffic for the web.
Mark Surman: So, I think we need to separate out what we want the web to be, or what the essence of what we wanted the web to be is, and the mechanics of how the web has been in its different eras.
So what did we want the web to be? It’s like a connection of all humanity, the ability to access all knowledge, the ability for that knowledge to be created democratically, the ability for us to find truth in that democratic creation of knowledge.
And so I think there’s a chance for all of that not only to continue to flourish in a world of AI, but to get better. But what we have to do now, if we want that to be true, is we need to reinvent how content is created. What do we mean by content? What does it mean to democratically influence it?
And most importantly, what are the business models? And if I go back to the beginning of the era of the open web, we were asking the same question: what are the business models? We’re all putting up websites.
Okay, now we have pop-up ads. Oh, now there are search engines with advertising. Oh, now there are apps that we can monetize. In that, we continue to have the democratic creation of knowledge.
We continue to actually know each other more. We continue to struggle over what truth is, but actually find our way in that. So I think it’s to always go back to those principles and then say: what are the new business models? What are the new delivery mechanisms?
What are the ways in which many of us can shape what the story is? And I think we’re just at a moment, and I say this on all of these fronts, that it’s very like the mid to late ’90s was for the web. We absolutely had a lot of hype. And people go, ‘Oh, the internet’s hype.’
Well, the internet mattered. The web mattered. But we didn’t actually know what it was for. We didn’t know how we were going to make money from it. We didn’t know who would get to share in the benefits.
And those should be the questions, as opposed to fearing that we lose the mechanics of how we did it before. How do we keep the essence of how we did it before and reinvent ourselves for the mechanics that this new fabric of technology creates.
3. Rising bot traffic and the need for new web architecture
Nikhil Pahwa: So I spoke with Matthew Prince, the co-founder of Cloudflare, a few days ago, and he said that about 35% of the web’s traffic currently is bots.
So one, assuming that maybe in the next year, year and a half, that might be more than 50%. It could go up to 99%. Who knows? It could go up to 99%.
So how do you think the architecture of the web needs to change? Do we need to have a separate agentic web? Do we need a new DNS for the web, for agents to basically navigate? Technically, how should the web change?
And in that sense, in an agent-first world, what technical properties have to exist for the web to remain open?
Mark Surman: The question of what technical properties need to exist is one that we’re going to be working through for a few years now. If I start with some of the things that I think are most important now, one of them is what our CTO, Rafi Kerkorian, talks about, is portable private memory.
And so this idea that right now, I take my context or my memory or my questions or my knowledge, and I build it up in, let’s say, ChatGPT. It gets better at understanding me.
We start to have, in many ways, a kind of interdependent relationship, where it becomes more helpful to me and it becomes more adapted to me. But if I decide to move to Gemini or Mistral, I’ve got to start over.
And so, purely from a consumer perspective or a human perspective, that’s a pain. And from a competition perspective and a value-control perspective, it’s kind of unfair, right?
I should be able to switch. I should be able to still be me across multiple models and multiple products. And so one thing that I think we need a standard architecture for is me. How do I control and own my memory
And then how do I build in sets of security and permission layers that allow me to control the relationships with what other agents can do in relation to my “me-ness,” and the permissions of how my agentic framework interacts with the rest of the agents in the world?
And so maybe that sounds like science fiction. I would say people have been talking about this for 20 years. It sounded like science fiction 20 years ago.
But in a world of OpenClaw or Claude Code work or all of these things, I think it’s increasingly clear we need this version of ourselves to be based in technical standards and in product.
And then it needs to have a set of negotiation properties, actually, as much as security properties.
So what that means then, as a person who is a source of content, a consumer of content, maybe also, if I imagine that portable private memory as something that can instantiate an entity, a publisher, a company, we have ways to negotiate value exchange and negotiate rights as we move through the system.
So I think that may sound very abstract, but there are people working on it, there are companies working on it, we’re working on it, and I think that will be a key piece of the fabric of what I think of as many of the properties of what we value in the web being protected.
4. Whether an agentic web requires new infrastructure
Nikhil Pahwa: Again, if we go beyond 50%, do we need a separate architecture of the web for agents?
Mark Surman: I don’t think we know yet. I think this is one component of an architecture we need. Does that still sit on top of TCP/IP? Does it still sit on top of DNS?
Those have been very resilient layers to build on. What underlying layers of the stack would need to change? I don’t think we’ll know the answers to that.
What I would say is, I don’t think we’ll know the answers to that for many years. What I think we need to remember is that the internet is this set of layers that has evolved over time, and one of its great properties is that technologies from one era have been able to function as a substrate for the technologies of the next.
So I believe we should move forward with that assumption for now. And then there may be places where we need to do massive renovation lower in the stack.
But for now, let’s assume we can get this done with TCP/IP, with DNS, and that we need to figure out things higher in the stack, things like my portable private memory.
And maybe we’ll have to come back and renovate some of those later, like we have with IPv6. But that is something that necessarily has to happen more slowly or incrementally, given how much we rely on it. This runs our whole world right now, the internet.
5. Whether browsers have a future in an AI agent-driven world
Nikhil Pahwa: So, I mean, the way I see it was, especially with the advent of social media and apps, thankfully deep linking was there and the browser still remained a mechanism for browsing the open web.
As agents become, or AI chatbots become, the default UI, what becomes of the browser? Is there a decline in the usage of browsers with increasing usage of chatbots? Apart from the fact that you can also open some of these services within chat, does the browser have a future when agents can browse the web for you, bring back information, and share it with you?
Mark Surman: It’s a good question, does the browser have a future?
At least the near-term answer is quite clear. OpenAI, Perplexity, and others are building browsers. We’re actually hearing many people say we’re in the new browser wars.
So I would say the browser is actually back on the table as a really important category for how we shape our interaction with the digital world. So the real question I would put forward to you or to people who are thinking about this, what do we want a browser to be?
I think there are two interesting points of contestation. One is: what do we want the browser to be? And we can talk about that. And then the other is: who is going to control and who is going to win this third browser war, if we think of it that way?
6. Browser engines, competition, and the need to evolve
Nikhil Pahwa: Well, there are essentially three browser engines in the world. You have one of them.
In that sense, do you think the browser engines need to evolve? Is there room for a fourth construct right now?
Mark Surman: Well, I think most people don’t know what a browser engine is. So just to say, a browser engine is the thing that sits underneath a browser that makes it able to interact with the web.
And we have one at Mozilla. Chromium is the most dominant one, made by Google, and Apple has its own.
I think there are both opportunities for others, and there’s a need for them to evolve. And I think you’re already seeing that with Chromium, and we’re thinking about it with Gecko, our engine.
And that means exactly engaging with the technical question: what will the web become, and what do we want it to become?
So looking at how Gecko evolves to hold as many of the properties of the web as we can, but also build in the ability for us to have control as, increasingly, the browser becomes our agent, and maybe that’s the important concept to put out there.
As the browser becomes our agent, or our central agent through which we interact with others, I don’t know that anybody has the map of what needs to change in browser engines versus what needs to change in the product.
But if you think about it that way, which is, if the browser becomes a container for me, or becomes my agent in the world, we always called the browser the “user agent” in a technical way.
And there are literally thousands, hundreds of thousands, millions of automated systems, other agents, coming at me. Its function is to be my trusted negotiator, its representation of myself.
What more important product category could there be than that?
7. Interoperability, agents, and the changing role of the browser
Nikhil Pahwa: So here’s one way I look at it. What agents are doing right now is almost forcing interoperability which had been otherwise walled or closed.
And so you prefer surfaces where agents can actually interact and you can engage with them. So the browser can actually become independent of the user interface that we currently have and become an agent that you can operate across Telegram, across a browser.
So the browser, as a current construct, remains one of the interfaces that the engine can call for you.
Mark Surman: And I think that’s absolutely the key piece, right? The browser is a new category. I think who’s going to win is a question, and then what we mean by a browser is another question.
And I don’t think that the form factor is what matters. I think the fact that it represents you, that it’s your place of safety, it’s your place of memory.
If you think about one of the things that Firefox is good at, it’s the place where the history of everything that you’ve seen digitally exists, and you can keep it privately.
Imagine extending that out into all of your digital interactions. That property is what matters in the browser, or your personal agent, as a user construct.
Whether or not you type into a URL bar or get search results as opposed to some chatbot result, that’s not what matters. That’s the evolution of what a browser will be.
And so, how do we engage with that concretely? Because we have to dive into these questions as products and as technology, right?
Nikhil Pahwa: Almost a browser as a second brain, so to speak.
Mark Surman: Or as an agent who represents me, right? Which it always has been, in many ways, but now it really matters.
And I think one of the things you’re saying, that agents are, in some ways, forcing interoperability.
But what they’re risking, and what we also need to balance, is trust, control, and security.
And so that’s where, if I think about what a browser is five years from now, it’s the thing that lets me interoperate with the digital world, other people, and also all the digital manifestations of those people and entities. And it also allows me to have and negotiate trust, security, and control. We need those things to continue to be humans in this hyper-scaled digital environment.
8. Mozilla’s values in an AI-driven internet
Nikhil Pahwa: And that actually makes Mozilla even more important, because as an organization, those values have defined Mozilla for many decades. And many of us still prefer Mozilla for that very reason.
Mark Surman: Well, I hope we’re up to the challenge. you need to bring those values.
So values like openness, values like privacy, although I think what that means is going to evolve, values like user control, values like security, we have to figure out what those mean now in a radically different digital world.
We’re committed to that. We’ve reorganized the whole organization to tackle that challenge.
And it’s a tricky game that we can only play with others. So I think now we just have to dig in, build things, and hold to our values. And our commitment is to do that. But it’s not a straightforward path.
9. Search, AI, and Mozilla’s Revenue Risk
Nikhil Pahwa: Well, you’ve had one scare in the last few years, which is the antitrust case against Google. And I’m saying, while this is important, the fact that search is collapsing into invocation, so to speak, is, if search collapses or reduces, or if it shifts to other answer engines, Mozilla’s finances are still largely tied to Google Search, right? So if the browser’s role changes, if people use browsers less, then what will Mozilla do?
Mark Surman: It’s very clear to us that the real economic risk for Mozilla wasn’t, or at least wasn’t primarily, the antitrust case. It’s that search changes, and that so much of our revenue, until recently about 95–98% of it, came from search, not just from Google, but also Bing and DuckDuckGo, and you can make those choices in Firefox.
And so, for a number of years, this has been a big part of my agenda in terms of how we’re changing Mozilla. Our goal is to diversify our revenue, and not just diversify our revenue in terms of who our business partners are or where our revenue comes from, but even the types of revenue.
And so one of the things that has happened is, you’ve seen Firefox move into, and it’s not a very AI-centric piece, it’s just a very basic thing in diversifying our revenue, advertising that is not based on search. Advertising that actually is the best-in-class in the industry on privacy, where you see advertising directly in Firefox.
And so we think advertising is still going to fund a part of how the internet goes forward, but you want to do that in a user-respecting way, and you don’t want to just tie it to search.
And so that has now become about 10–12% of our revenue. So we’re probably now down to about 85% coming from search. So that’s the first step.
But as we move into, we’ve been setting up new companies and new investment mechanisms in other areas. We’ve built an open-source AI developer tools company, hopefully that becomes a source of enterprise revenue. Or we are building a two-sided data marketplace for ethical training data, hopefully that becomes a way to actually shift the data marketplace, but also a source of revenue.
So we are very actively looking for both other ways that we can influence the direction of the web and AI, and other ways to make money.
And I think the two things go together, right? I mean, you have to actually be in the market as well as sit there as a nonprofit in order to influence the direction of this stuff. We’ve always been about both things.
And that ties both to our mission and to how our money works.
10. Product Strategy and Organisational Shift
Nikhil Pahwa: So you’ve also, you now have Mozilla VPN, you’ve also tried multiple, you’ve launched products but sunset them eventually. You bought products like Pocket and then sunset them.
I’m just curious about how you think about products within Mozilla, and why it is that great products like Lockwise and Pocket no longer exist?
Mark Surman: The thing I would point out, one of the things that I would say we have a much different focus on now in terms of products, is focus. And that doesn’t mean only doing one thing, but it means having different leaders to do different things.
I think we’ve tried to shove too many things under one leader and kind of maybe we’re doing a million things plus Firefox, but then we’re just doing Firefox.
And if I’m honest, that part of the product focus has been difficult, to get the right setup and the right leadership for.
But what we have now is the core Firefox organization, that’s one company in our portfolio. It has a new CEO who’s amazing, Anthony DeMaio. And he’s really looking at how we evolve Firefox, diversify revenue, and offerings inside of the Firefox brand.
And so VPN will be coming under Firefox and will be integrated into it. As we figure out new AI features, they’ll be a part of AI mode in Firefox.
So I think a really strong, technologically savvy CEO leading the Firefox product group, really focused on how Firefox evolves and changes with AI, that is a thing we’ve never really locked before, and I think we have now.
But then, in the Thunderbird group, there’s their own, it’s its own company. It has its own CEO. They’re looking at how we evolve private email and what’s beyond email.
In the AI developer tools space, which we always had developer tooling tied to Firefox, we’re building a separate company there.
I think one of the things that’s just radically different in our structure is dedicated product companies in different product categories, with their own leaders, their own business plans, their own P&Ls
And so, who knows if we’ll succeed, but we have a very different approach than we’ve had in the last 15 years.
11. Mozilla Ventures and the “Firefox-like” Model
Nikhil Pahwa: What’s the thought process around the investment vehicle? Are you investing for exits? Are you investing for something that can grow Mozilla’s own products? Or what’s the idea behind it? Or can you take something to an IPO?
Mark Surman: So when you say the investment vehicle, you mean the ones I just mentioned or our venture fund?
Nikhil Pahwa: I’m talking about the venture fund.
Mark Surman: Yeah. So the theory of the venture fund is really very simple, we want to see more Firefox-like things in the world.
So, products based on our values that influence the market because they’re popular, and the side effect is they make money. And so that’s what Firefox is to us.
And we’ve tried to make more Firefoxes, and maybe we will. I think we’re going to just make the one Firefox we have better and evolve it in the AI world.
And we’re trying to set up some of our own companies to create more Firefox-like things. But the odds of us having a second hit record, those are low odds.
But we want a lot more people to have that kind of hit record.
And so Mozilla Ventures is just about backing founders who are building Firefox like products.
The idea behind Mozilla Ventures is really that we’re backing founders who might create these Firefox-like things, responsible tech products that have our values and are commercial.
And so, in that, we have people who are doing developer-oriented products that are radically different approaches to AI architecture. So there’s a federated learning company called Flower that I love, that could really change the whole architecture of AI.
Or we have AI governance companies in there that, if and when things start going wrong with AI, are going to be really important companies. Like, you know, there’s one called Credo AI, which is kind of a leader in AI governance.
And we’re also starting to see consumer tech companies that have a different view of social media or a different view of how we all work together on the web.
12. What is Open-Source AI?
Nikhil Pahwa: Let’s talk about open source. What is open-source AI, and what is not open-source AI?
Mark Surman: What is open-source AI is a hotly debated question, although, in many ways, not many people are actually in that hot debate. And so, to kind of bring it into the nerdiness of the people who do talk about this:
Open source at its core is about four things. It’s that I can use it freely. It’s that I can study it, that it’s transparent, that I can look inside how it works. I can modify it, I can change it for myself, I can build something new from it. And then I can redistribute that, I can sell it or I can give it away to somebody else.
That’s the essence of what open source is. And that’s how Firefox and Linux and so many things have grown, right? Because I can do those things.
So many people use it, so many people build on it and make it better, so many people spread it again, and it takes over the whole world.
So ultimately, open-source AI is the things that have those properties. And ultimately, I think they can take over the whole world. I think open source can be the winning paradigm if we get it right in AI.
And so, many of those properties are in the open source, or the things that get called open source but are really open-weight models, whether that is something like LLaMA, or something like Qwen, something like Mistral.
And then more importantly, or I think the thing that people miss, is many of the things that matter in the rest of the open-source AI stack: the things we use to train models, the things we use to pull data, the things we use to orchestrate, are open source in the traditional sense.
So what I would say is there’s a lot of open-source activity in AI, to the point where I could build, in fact, we have built, many people have built, their own ChatGPT-like things in mostly open-source ways.
And so, three years ago, you couldn’t have imagined that. Now, there are thousands of them.
The one limitation, or the one thing that really is a sticking point, is that most of the things that get called open-source models, like LLaMA or Qwen, the underlying training process, what the data is, how it was trained, the pre-training, is opaque. And that is not open source in any traditional sense, in that I can’t really look inside that part.
So those things, I think they’re still incredibly valuable. We use them, we build on them. They’re better than something that’s just locked behind an API that I can’t modify on top of.
But I think there is a real question: can we get to the point of highly performant, what people often call truly open models, where I see what the training data is, what the pre-training checkpoints were, and can I modify them and fork them at that level?
And I think, in the current architecture of LLMs as one type of AI, that is a harder thing because there’s so much money that goes into that process.
And what you’re seeing more is open source in relation to models being open source in terms of the ability to take them freely, modify them dramatically, and then be able to choose between and orchestrate multiple models.
So, just to take a case: if I pull together something on top of LLaMA or Qwen, and then I do fine-tuning or transform it in some way, or combine it with other models and create my own new small model and put it under a very good open-source license, some kind of Apache or MIT license, is that thing open source or not?
Not totally, truly, because the thing underneath is opaque. But I’ve changed it so much and made it something new, and taken these different pieces of raw material, I can say how I did that. Maybe it is.
And so I think, in that part of the creation and transformation of models, it’s almost like the materiality of that part of the stack, we have to wrestle with what we mean by open source.
And I think, frankly, my instinct is it’s going in the right direction, but we’re still babies in this, and we don’t quite know what we mean.
13. Licensing, Commons, and Corporate Use
Nikhil Pahwa: So one fear that I’ve seen from the open-source community in India that works on AI is around licensing, saying that if they truly build a completely open AI model, then all of that information, the weights, the data, will just be taken by proprietary large language models.
So they’re struggling with figuring out how to create a license for this.
Mark Surman: That doesn’t seem like a licensing question as much as a ‘do you believe in open source or not’ question.
Nikhil Pahwa: I think they want to be useful, but they don’t want to empower the proprietary system at the same time.
Mark Surman: But open source has always done that. I mean, the way that open source works is that anybody can use it, anybody can modify it, anybody can share it, right?
And so that becomes the question of how do you balance having a commons versus corporate benefit, or arguments about corporate capture.
So you can have a debate, but I don’t think it’s any different in AI, or whether it’s a good thing or a bad thing that the whole tech industry and most governments run on the Linux ecosystem, run on the Linux stack.
I think that’s good. We have a common infrastructure for humanity that individuals, companies, and governments all contribute to in different ways.
It’s highly leveraged when you think about the overall open-source ecosystem. The chief economist of the Linux Foundation has shown, Frank Nagle, that there’s only about $4 billion to create most of the open-source stack that we have now, and about $8 trillion in value that is unlocked from that.
So you have this highly leveraged commons, and yes, of course, companies benefit from it, but they are also the biggest contributors back to it.
And if that is how the AI infrastructure of the world works, that it’s a commons that, yes, companies use, but I can use too, and that they contribute to it as well, that’s kind of a better version of the economics of a thing than we have in most other parts of society.
I’m happy to make that trade.
14. Mozilla’s Approach to Open AI Ecosystems
Nikhil Pahwa: Just on openness, I’m curious, because we’ve seen a few things happen in the last many years. For example, the advent of F-Droid as an open-source app store, the advent of Hugging Face as a source for open-source models.
Has Mozilla considered either integrating, working? Is there an easy or quick way that I can implement a small language model within the browser itself?
Have you had those conversations? Have you thought of going down that path of becoming the open space where the open AI ecosystem sort of congregates, and the app ecosystem congregates? Because not everyone knows about F-Droid, but the browser is a great way for discovery of that integration.
Mark Surman: That’s a good question. It’s like, what’s the role of the browser, and what’s the role of Mozilla? And I would separate those in creating, basically, optionality and a way for there to be an open ecosystem of people pulling in different things, different agents, different models, different digital objects.
And we absolutely thought of that.
I will say, the first step in that is Mozilla AI, the separate company we spun out for developers. Its focus is on exactly that kind of choice.
So it’s putting out libraries like “any LLM” or “any agent,” which are basically developer tools oriented toward building dynamic choice into the architecture of what you do.
So it means that I can test against five different models. I can build an actual production-grade system that dynamically switches between models based on different parameters, including cost.
And if we start to build that as an architecture of choice, and start to build that out as standards in how developers work, then you’re going to start to have some of the substrate of what you’re talking about, which is choice. Choice in how AI works in the underlying architecture.
And we need that first, and we need that to emerge in the market amongst developers before it can really be practical.
And so that’s the first step we’re doing, and we’re trying to build a public-benefit business around that in the developer space.
How does that then show up for consumers, which the browser is the obvious vector for that? I think actually the email client might be another place for that.
I think we’re talking about it. The path is not yet quite clear. The values are clear.
So as we build out AI mode in Firefox, which we haven’t talked about yet, it being a place where I have agency to choose different services, combine things, I have choice, I can use different models, I can have my own controlled memory, all of those things are a part of what we’re talking about there.
Does it actually look like a Hugging Face or an F-Droid, or connect you to those things? I think we’re too early to know exactly how those kinds of things would work out.
15. Takeaways from the India AI Impact Summit
Nikhil Pahwa: We’ve just finished our last day of the AI Summit in India. What were your takeaways from the summit? There’s been some amount of criticism about the organization of the summit, but what was the sense of the vibe, of the conversations, of what you’ve seen in Delhi?
Mark Surman: I mean, I love Delhi. I’ve been here many times, and the summit and Delhi kind of fused in a really wonderful way. It feels like a really vibrant city where a lot of different collisions happen.
AI was everywhere, and it was a little chaotic, but beautiful.
And so I would say two things about the summit.
One is, you had the right set of players here, or at least enough of the right types of players here, in terms of real political power and leadership, real leadership in the tech industry of different kinds and competing kinds, not just the big American players, although they were also here, and civil society voices and startups and a lot of the smaller players.
What a summit like this I judge on is not what’s the announcement that comes out of it, it’s what are the conversations, the deals, and the kind of “banging up against each other” that happened.
And I think the Delhi summit set the perfect context for that and brought together an amazing group of people.
On specifics, what I would say is there was a conversation that was more exciting and real to me about there being what people often talk about, a middle powers way or a third way on AI, and that open source really is an option.
On substance, what I would say is there really was this conversation about open source, tied to this middle-powers idea, or that the tech industry needn’t just be the US and China defining everything.
Of course, there are leading players and really important players that everybody wants to work with, but countries that may think they’re further behind, or think they’re too small, whether that’s India, or Canada where I’m from, or anywhere else, that we can shape part of the agenda, and that we can capture a part of the value.
And that open source is actually the key ingredient to that.
And it’s the key ingredient because people see it’s capable enough now in AI to be useful. It’s something where I can do one component and connect it to all the other components, and capture value for myself and have influence for myself without having to build the whole thing.
It’s 80% done already, and so I get a head start.
And I think, in a way that I haven’t seen before, that conversation, that potential of not just relying on America and China as the only drivers of the future, but that we can all shape the future and all benefit economically and socially from the future, that spirit, and concrete examples and plans on that, at a business level, at a government level, those were really happening in all the conversations here.
Or at least the conversations I was in.
Nikhil Pahwa: There were many conversations.
Mark Surman: Yes, there were many conversations that I wasn’t paying attention to or wasn’t in, which were not that.
But that was palpable, that open source can create a space for a different kind of AI and a different set of people to benefit, other than those who think they’re in charge right now.
16. Small Language Models vs Big AI
Nikhil Pahwa: So one conversation that I felt was missing at the summit, and maybe I missed those conversations if they did happen, was the focus on small language models.
It almost seems as if large language models are afraid of company, and that’s all we will talk about, whether it is open-sourced or not.
Do you think that’s a conversation we need to have more of? Or do you think there’s no role for that, because large persistent memory linked to large language models is the way the world is shifting today?
Mark Surman: What I would say is, you were in the wrong conversations.
So I was in many conversations about small language models. I mean, you saw a bunch of announcements and a keynote by Sarah Hooker, who is the founder of this new startup called Adaption, which I should say we’re an investor in.
And it’s very much about how do we have adaptive small language models as a new paradigm that is more targeted at our needs, more affordable, more efficient in terms of compute.
I was then on other panels with leading AI researchers who were saying, “2026, this is going to be the year of small language models.”
There was a lot of that in the right rooms.
The people who are on the main stage don’t benefit if this becomes the year of small language models, but it will be.
And I think small language models are kind of adjacent to open source, because the open models are actually raw material for small language models.
And so I think that open source, small language models, orchestrating many models together, how that connects to agents, that is where things are going.
I think the absolutely big, monolithic, centralized models, they will play a central role, but they will not be the deciding or defining part of the architecture of this going forward.
17. Mozilla’s India Strategy and Re-engagement
Nikhil Pahwa: Just from an India perspective, Mozilla is one of the most credible voices, with the advocacy work that you’ve done for the open web. You had two great people in India, Amba Kak, who now heads the AI Now Institute, and Udbhav Tiwari, whom you first moved to Berlin.
A few years ago, it almost felt like Mozilla had largely backed away from India. And I have heard from people in the community that they feel a bit abandoned in India.
India has contributed to Mozilla and has deep love for Mozilla. I’m just curious about what plans you may have for India. This is the largest internet market in the world in terms of users outside of China, and we hear big tech companies coming in and making announcements.
But they come with their own agenda, and I’m just wondering what Mozilla has in store for India.
Mark Surman: Well, I would say it’s fair, not that we abandoned India, but I think, as we went through some of our leadership changes, we just didn’t have, especially on the Firefox side, the engagement with the community for a few years that we once had.
And I think that’s honestly a little bit of us finding ourselves.
But I’ll say two things.
On the developer front, we really want to re-engage with India. Our CTO, Rafi Kerkorian, is in Bangalore right now while we’re here, doing developer meetups with open-source developers there.
And that’s meant to be a first kind of handshake to say: we’re back, and we want to know what you want to do.
I think we’re particularly looking for people in India who are doing open-source AI startups, open-source AI projects, how can we help?
So I would say that anybody who fits that description and wants to work with us, please reach out to me and Rafi. That is a big area where we see potential and action in India.
And then, as Firefox adapts under its new leadership, India is at the top of the list of places where we know we need to put more resources.
India feels like a place that has always embraced Firefox. Firefox was always the thing that first had more Indian languages than the other browsers. And why did it have that? Because of the Indian Firefox community, they made those translations.
And so you’re going to see more engagement from Firefox in India.
Just because we are trying to rebuild the proposition of the browser, we think India is a place where people will be interested in what it means in this AI era to have a browser that gives you choice, that gives you control, that isn’t just telling you, “This is the one way that AI can work,” but lets you shape how AI is going to work.
So I think both on the developer side and on the Firefox side, we’re very interested in coming back and re-engaging. And hopefully people will want to engage and trust us.
But we know there is a bit of a sense that we stepped out of the picture for a moment.
18. India’s Opportunity in the AI Transition
Nikhil Pahwa: We’re sort of in an online-to-offline moment here, almost an online-to-AI transition. AI is changing the world, and I’ve seen a lot of it. I mostly was in founder conversations and the parties where founders were congregating, and there is a lot of networking.
I got a sense of people wanting to work together or collaborate together.
So we are going to see more things getting built out of here, more things happening here.
And I would love for Mozilla to be the moral compass of how the web transitions to AI, if it indeed does do that.
Mark Surman: Well, I think the thing I would say is India has a tremendous opportunity, and Indian startups and Indian open-source developers have an opportunity, to influence that question themselves: where does the web go, and how do the things that are valuable about the web and the internet get embodied in and shape the direction of AI?
And so I think of us more as somebody who can help and partner to guide that direction.
But I think there’s a real chance for India, Indian companies, Indian open-source developers, to actually have influence on what that is, and influence in the world.
And that’s the conversation we want to have.

articles.summary:
Mozilla President Mark Surman argues that the open web can survive the rise of AI by evolving browsers into "agentic" tools that prioritize user control and portable private memory.

### Key Points
- Surman emphasizes that browsers must evolve into "user agents" that act as trusted negotiators for security, privacy, and data ownership.
- Mozilla is actively diversifying its revenue beyond search, with non-search advertising now accounting for 10–12% of its income.
- The organization is spinning off dedicated companies, such as Mozilla AI, to focus on open-source developer tools and ethical data marketplaces.
- Surman identifies "portable private memory" as a critical technical standard to prevent users from being locked into a single AI model.
- Mozilla is prioritizing re-engagement with India’s developer community to foster open-source AI projects and influence the future of the web.

### Why it Matters
As AI-driven interfaces threaten traditional web traffic and search-based business models, Mozilla’s strategy highlights the urgent need for new infrastructure that protects user autonomy. By championing open-source standards and interoperability, the organization aims to prevent a future where AI ecosystems are controlled exclusively by a few proprietary, closed-source entities.
